info.name: Nelvora Items
info.description: Custom items for the Nelvora setting.
info.author: RiverDoge

description.p: A specialized vessel utilized by the Utsuwa. This particular Fuin-Sho has the capacity to bind and hold up to three distinct Magatsu.

This ring is crafted from a band of dark, matte metal. A tiny, intricately detailed silver serpent wraps around the band, its jaws stretched wide. Clutched within the serpent's fangs is a pale, glowing gemstone that flickers erratically, perfectly mimicking the light of a dying torch. The metal is warm to the touch and hums with a faint, deceptive heartbeat.

Veil of False Hope. As a reaction when you are targeted by an attack, you can command the gemstone to flare with a blinding flash. You instantly turn invisible, reappearing 5 feet adjacent to your current position, and a perfect, tangible illusory duplicate of yourself takes your original place to absorb the blow. The attack automatically hits the duplicate instead of you, causing the illusion to shatter into a cloud of glowing, harmless sparks. You remain invisible until the start of your next turn, or until you make an attack or cast a spell. Once you use this reaction, you cannot use it again until a week has passed.

Warped Truth. When a creature attempts to read your thoughts, determine your alignment, or force you to tell the truth, you may make a Charisma roll to contest this attempt. On a success, you can choose to feed them only fragments of information, slightly shifting information like alignment over one step (Good to neutral). The greater you bend the truth, the more likely they are to know what you have done. You may make this roll even if you fail any other saving throw related to these former effects (zone of truth for instance).

The Shifting Path. The ring allows you to twist the environment to reflect a different version of events. You can use an action to cast Pass without Trace or Nystul's Magic Aura from the ring without providing components. Once you cast either spell, you cannot do so again until a week has passed.

This intricate ring is forged from an unknown, star-like metal that seems to absorb and hold light. It was found nestled in the silt at the bottom of a forgotten hamlet's lake, untouched by rust or time and radiating a faint, analytical chill. The three crystalline sockets set into the band are perfectly empty, yet flicker with a nervous, predictive potential, as if waiting to hold something they were always meant to contain.
The ring has 3 charges, which it uses for its Bestow Fate feature. It regains all expended charges daily at dawn.

The ring can store the distilled essence of fortune and misfortune. It can hold up to 3 Fateful Motes at one time, which are either Calamitous (captured from a natural 1) or Triumphant (captured from a natural 20). Any motes currently stored in the ring dissipate when you finish a long rest.

Capture Fate. As a reaction, when a creature (including yourself) you can see within 60 feet of you rolls a natural 1 or a natural 20 on an attack roll, saving throw, or ability check, you can attempt to capture the roll. You cannot use this reaction if the ring is already holding 3 Fateful Motes.

ul.li: If the triggering roll was a natural 20: The creature's roll is nullified. That creature must immediately reroll the d20, and it does so with disadvantage. You then store one Triumphant Mote in the ring.
If the triggering roll was a natural 1: The creature's roll is nullified. That creature must immediately reroll the d20, and it does so with advantage. You then store one Calamitous Mote in the ring.

description.p: Bestow Fate. As a reaction, when a creature (including yourself) you can see within 60 feet of you makes an attack roll, saving throw, or ability check, you can expend 1 charge and one stored Fateful Mote (Calamitous or Triumphant) from the ring to replace that creature's d20 roll.

ul.li: Willing Target: If the target is willing, their d20 roll is replaced by the outcome of the mote (a natural 1 for Calamitous, a natural 20 for Triumphant).

Unwilling Target: If the target is unwilling, they must make a DC 18 Intelligence saving throw. On a failed save, their d20 roll is replaced with the outcome of the mote. On a successful save, the charge and the mote are both expended to no effect.

description.p: Skew Fortune. As a reaction, when a creature (including yourself) you can see within 60 feet of you makes an attack roll, saving throw, or ability check, you can roll a 1d4. You choose to either add or subtract the number rolled from the creature's d20 roll. You can use this reaction after the creature's d20 is rolled, but before the DM determines the outcome. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus. You regain all expended uses when you finish a long rest.

These are a pair of expertly crafted handwraps, made not from cloth or bark, but from the first shed leaves of Ormaldë, the World Tree, gathered in the season of your birth. Your mother wove them for you, painstakingly curing each leaf with the tree's own sacred sap, creating a material that is softer than silk yet stronger than cured leather.

They are a deep, living green, and they feel cool and vibrant to the touch. They are a piece of your very origin, a direct connection to the heart of Farangroth. When you channel your ki, the faint, golden veins in each leaf glow with a soft light, and the wraps rustle as if touched by a wind from Ormaldë's highest branches.
Súryanda's Swiftness. While wearing these wraps, you gain a +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls with your unarmed strikes. Your unarmed strikes are considered magical.

Wind's Embrace. Your Súryanda heritage and the wraps' connection to the winds of home make you unnaturally fast. While you are wearing these wraps and are not wearing any armor or using a shield, your base walking speed increases by 10 feet.

Whispering Gale. When you spend at least 1 Ki point as part of your action or bonus action on your turn, the wraps channel the winds of Farangroth around you. Until the end of that turn, your movement does not provoke opportunity attacks.

Sapling's Tenacity. Your bond to the World Tree provides you with a deep, resilient vitality. As a bonus action, you can spend 1 Ki point to awaken this energy, regaining a number of hit points equal to 1d10 + your Wisdom modifier. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a long rest.

This simple hairband is woven from a silken, alabaster fabric that remains unnervingly pristine. No matter how much blood, mud, or ash touches it, the material sheds the filth instantly and returns to a stark, blinding white. The fabric is cool and damp, as if it has been soaked in holy water that never dries.
It was found binding the eyes of a statue in the tomb's inner sanctum, a statue whose face was worn smooth as if eroded by centuries of weeping.
Transcendent Reach. While wearing this fillet, your ki projects outward as visible, spectral limbs of pale light. The range of your Hands of Healing and Hands of Harm features increases to 30 feet.

Anoint the Wounded. Your touch carries the potent, ancient authority of purification. When you use Hands of Healing, you can choose to maximize the roll of your Martial Arts die instead of rolling it. Additionally, if you use your Physician’s Touch feature to end a disease or a condition on a creature, you also grant that creature Temporary Hit Points equal to your Wisdom modifier + your Monk level + the spell level of whatever was afflicting them.

The Vessel of Filth (Curse). This fillet belonged to a healer who believed that sickness could not be destroyed, only moved. This item is cursed. Attuning to it extends the curse to you. As long as you remain cursed, you cannot remove the fillet unless you are targeted by a Remove Curse spell or similar magic. The item demands that you become a vessel for the corruption you purge.

The Sin-Eater. Whenever you use Hands of Healing to restore hit points to a creature, you take Necrotic damage equal to one roll of your Martial Arts die. This damage cannot be reduced or prevented in any way.

Sympathetic Agony. Whenever you use your Physician’s Touch feature to end a condition (such as blinded, deafened, paralyzed, poisoned, or stunned) on a creature, you immediately contract that condition yourself. The condition lasts on you until the end of your next turn, regardless of its original duration.

This smoothly polished, teardrop-shaped slab is carved from an impossible, opalescent stone. Instead of ordinary paints, the mixing wells constantly swirl with liquid, glowing light in impossible, shifting spectrums. When you hold it, the air around you faintly smells of ozone, fresh rain, and crushed minerals.

Properties. When you attune to this primordial palette, you tap into the lingering colors of creation. You gain a +2 bonus to spell attack rolls and +1 to saving throw DCs of your spells while holding it. The palette holds 5 charges and regains 1d4 charges daily at dawn.

The Master's Pigment. When you use your Performance of Creation feature, you can expend 2 charges to paint a true masterpiece into reality. The item you create is flawlessly detailed, and the gold piece limit for the item is quadrupled. Furthermore, the item has resistance to all damage, and if you create a tool or a weapon, any creature using it gains a +1 bonus to ability checks or attack rolls made with it. Because of the sheer creative toll this takes, once you use this property, you cannot use it again until seven days have passed.

Animated Masterpiece. When you use your Animating Performance feature, you can expend 1 charge to drip primordial, starlit ink onto your creation. When your creation hits a creature with any attack, the target takes an extra 1d8 acid or radiant damage (your choice of color upon animating it). Additionally, the target is splashed with glowing paint, granting advantage on the next attack roll made against it before the start of your creations next turn.

Splash of Potential. When a creature rolls your Bardic Inspiration die and triggers your Mote of Potential feature, you can use your reaction and expend 1 charge to cause the mote to violently burst like a paint bomb. In addition to the normal Mote effect, choose one of the following:

ul.li: The creature rolling the die gains hit points equal to the number rolled.
One enemy within 5 feet of them must succeed on a Dexterity saving throw against your spell DC or be blinded until the start of its next turn.

description.p: The World in Strokes. As an action, you can hurl the palette's colors into the air, turning the battlefield into your canvas. You create a 30-foot-radius sphere of magical paint centered on a point you can see within 60 feet. For 1 minute (requires concentration), the ground in the area is covered in thick, wet paint, making it difficult terrain for your enemies. While you are concentrating on this effect, you have advantage on Constitution saving throws made to maintain your concentration. While the sphere persists, you can use your bonus action on each of your turns to manipulate the paint in one of the following ways:

ul.li: Highlight: You paint a bright, glowing outline every target of your choice that you can see within the sphere. The targets must succeed on a Dexterity saving throw against your spell save DC. On a failure, attack rolls against them have advantage until the end of your next turn.
Illustrate: You quickly sketch solid barriers around all allies within the sphere. They gain +2 AC until the end of this feature, and have the benefits of half-cover.

Splatter: You cause the paint on the ground to violently burst upward. Each enemy in the sphere must make a Constitution saving throw against your spell save DC, taking 4d6 acid or radiant damage (your choice) on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. Targets affected by Highlight have disadvantage on this spell save. Additionally, on a failed save, targets are marked with paint and take an additional 1d6 acid or radiant (based on previous choice) damage from all attacks, stacking for each failed save until the end of this feature.

Refinish: You erase a creature from the canvas and quickly paint them elsewhere. Choose one creature within the sphere, or choose everyone affected by highlight. An unwilling target must succeed on a Charisma saving throw against your spell save DC. On a failure (or if the creature is willing), the target is instantly teleported up to 30 feet to an unoccupied space you can see within the sphere.

description.p: Dormancy. Once the minute passes, or if your concentration is broken, the magic rapidly dries and flakes away into dust. The palette immediately goes dormant. For exactly seven days, it becomes a mundane stone slab. It loses its +2 bonus to spell attacks and save DCs, its remaining charges vanish, and it cannot regain charges or be used for any magical properties until the seventh dawn has passed.

This light strip of ash-grey silk feels cool to the touch. In the very center of the fabric rests the image of a closed eye, meticulously crafted from intertwining threads of bright silver and dull gold. When you tie the cloth across your face, the magic within activates. To anyone looking at you, the metallic threads of the eye seem to slowly open, shifting to depict an endless, spiraling void that weaves endlessly inward.

Properties. When you tie this shroud over your eyes, the world around you goes black, subjecting you to the Blinded condition. However, as the cold silk settles against your skin, a new and unerring perception blooms in your mind.
The Sightless Gaze. You gain Blindsight out to a range of 60 feet. Within this radius, you navigate non-magical darkness perfectly, and you can clearly see invisible creatures or hidden objects.

Dull Horizon. While you can see at a range of 60 feet, that is the absolute limit of your vision. Anything beyond that range is effectively lost to the void at the edge of your vision. Additionally, any enemy attacking from beyond that range does so with advantage, and you automatically fail any purely sight based perception checks beyond 60 feet.

Blind to the Mundane and Benevolent. The woven eye seeks to reveal that which is born of deception, and those of a magical nature. Beings that are entirely mundane, either due to a severed connection to the arcane, or merely the complete lack of potential for it, are invisible to this sight. This of course also extends to reading books, or any other form of mundane writing; as you can only read text woven from magic, or non-mundane origins. Furthermore, this silken shroud turns a blind-eye towards beings of immense good. Namely this sight is incapable of perceiving overtly good beings, with the exception of a blinding radiance engulfing those of divine origin, or immensely good alignment (usually servants of deities, or those who have done an overwhelming amount of good in their life).

The Shroud of Magic. When you enter an area of magical darkness, the heavy weave of the spell creates static in your mind. You can still see through the magical darkness, but the world appears hazy and blurred. You treat areas of magical darkness as lightly obscured.

Piercing the Veil. You strike with absolute certainty against those who hide their true nature. Any attack roll against a creature that is magically disguised, shape changed, or under the effects of an illusion is made with advantage, provided you are aware of their deception with any knowledge of their true identity.

Decree of the Nameless. Once per week, you can use an action to speak a single word of absolute law. You cast the Zone of Truth spell (Save DC 16) centered on yourself. Furthermore, any creature within the zone that fails its Charisma saving throw instantly drops any illusion magic disguising its appearance.

This heavy pendant is forged from dark, purplish iron and framed by a pristine golden trim. At its center rests a polished obsidian core that beats with a slow, rhythmic thrum. Six distinct, limb like tendrils wrap around the iron frame. All six point inward, their sharp tips digging into the obsidian as if dragging something into the depths.

Properties. When you wear this necklace, you become a locus for the magic of a forgotten God. The pendant holds up to 6 charges, represented by the six dark-iron tendrils. It begins each day 0 charges and loses all stored charges daily at dawn.

The Soul Tithe. Whenever a hostile creature of Challenge Rating 1 or higher dies within 60 feet of you, the obsidian core pulses. One of the iron tendrils glows with a faint, golden heat, and the pendant gains 1 charge. If this effect triggers while there is already 6 charges, you instead regain 1d10 hit points.

Fetter of the Grave. You can expend 1 charges as a bonus action to command the stone. A spectral iron tendril erupts from the ground beneath a creature you can see within 30 feet of you. The target must succeed on a DC 16 Strength saving throw or be restrained until the end of your next turn. While restrained in this way, the heavy necrotic magic prevents the creature from regaining hit points. This ability can be used three times per short-rest.

Weight of the Fallen. You can use an action and expend 2 charges to unleash the crushing pressure of the grave. Each creature of your choice within 20 feet of you must make a DC 16 Strength saving throw. On a failure, a creature takes 5d8 necrotic damage and is violently pulled up to 15 feet directly toward you. On a successful save, the creature takes half as much damage and is not pulled. This ability can be used once per short-rest.

Deny the Abyss. The sovereign of death claims their due only when they choose. If you are reduced to 0 hit points and the pendant has at least 2 charges stored, you can command the stone to release its stolen essence in your final moments. The glowing tendrils detach from the stone and plunge into your chest. You expend all stored charges, instantly regaining 1d10 hit points per charge expended, and you remain conscious. Once you use this ability, the core goes completely cold, and you cannot use this specific property again until seven days have passed.
Black, split-toed socks with soles that always leave a faint, damp footprint, even on dry stone.

Surface Tension. You can move across fluid surfaces as if they were harmless solid ground, as long as you keep moving at a calm pace. This means you do not incur any effects such as poison, acid, or other types of hazards from moving through such surfaces, unless you fall through.

Sanctity of Self. If you are standing in an area created by a spell or effect you control, you ignore the Difficult Terrain and take no damage from the area's hazards. Additionally, you gain 10 feet of movement on any surface you create, and gain double benefit from any terrain or weather spell that improves movement.
Enchanted Cloth. You gain a +1 bonus to your AC while wearing this garment.

Shroud of Sorrow. When you are reduced to half hit points or fewer, the cloth will emit a thick mist that cloaks you in a shadow, granting you +2 AC for one minute, as well as advantage on Dexterity saving throws. This effect can only occur once per week.

Wail of the Forgotten. Once per long rest, you can unleash a mournful wail as an action. Each creature of your choice that you can see within 30 feet must make a Wisdom saving throw against a DC of 16. On a failed save, the target takes 3d6 psychic damage and is frightened until the end of your next turn. On a successful save, they take half damage and suffer none of the negative effects.

Mortuary of the Forgotten. When in the presence of one beloved by the Forgotten King, you may be overwhelmed by an unwieldy force, and temporarily possessed. Once this occurs, it can never happen again, but Sorrow's Guise will be forever changed by this monumental event.

These fingerless gloves are knitted from a strange, wool-like yarn that feels perpetually warm and soft, smelling faintly of woodsmoke and dried lavender. Despite their delicate, home-spun appearance, the fibers are uncuttable and refuse to stain, hinting at a more grandeous nature. When worn, they create a subtle, comforting pressure around the hands, like the reassuring grip of a close friend.

Nurturing Bond. While you are attuned to these gloves, your magic acts as a conduit for your own warmth and comfort. Whenever you cast a conjuration spell that summons or creates creatures (such as Find Familiar, Conjure Animals, or Summon Beast), those creatures gain the following benefits.

Heart of the Hearth. You generate a pool of Temporary Hit Points equal to your level + 10 times the level of the spell slot used. You must divide these temporary hit points evenly among the creatures summoned by the spell (rounded down).
Unshakable Trust. The creatures have advantage on Wisdom saving throws and are immune to the Psychological conditions, as they constantly feel your reassuring presence in their minds.
Guided Hand. While a summoned creature is within 60 feet of you, it adds your Proficiency Bonus to its attack rolls and damage rolls, guided by your bond.

The Burden of Loss (Curse). These gloves were created by a soul who loved too deeply to let go. This item is cursed. Attuning to it extends the curse to you. As long as you remain cursed, you cannot remove the gloves unless you are targeted by a Remove Curse spell or similar magic. The gloves tie your physical and emotional state to the existence of your conjurations. This connection anchors you to reality, but leaves you vulnerable to heartbreak.

Anchored by Love. While you have at least one summoned creature active, your attachment to them helps you to persist. You have advantage on all Constitution saving throws. This protection extends to any effect that would forcefully end concentration, such as any effect that would result in an incapacitation.

The Price of Grief. Whenever a creature you summoned drops to 0 hit points, is banished, or disappears because the spell ended early (such as losing concentration), you are overwhelmed by sudden, sharp heartbreak. You take 3d6 psychic damage per creature lost. This damage ignores resistance and immunity.

This rigid neck-ring is crafted from a matte, slate-grey metal that feels heavier than it looks. Suspended at the throat is a housing of clear, indestructible crystal containing a droplet of silvery liquid which defies gravity. It seems to swirl in complex, predictable orbits that match the pulse of the wearer.
The Shared Step. While attuned to this torque, you utilize the spatial bond between creator and creation to manipulate the battlefield.
Transposition. As a Bonus Action or reaction, you can teleport, swapping places with a creature you have summoned or created via a Conjuration spell, provided the creature is within 60 feet of you.

Far Becomes Close. When you cast a spell with a range of Touch, you can choose to have the spell originate from one of your summoned creatures as if you were standing in its space. Your summon acts as the conduit for your magic.

Constrained Ward. The bond that tethers you also sustains you. At the start of each of your turns, you and any of your summoned creatures within 60 feet of you gain Temporary Hit Points equal to 5 + your Proficiency Bonus.

The Invisible Chain (Curse). This torque was forged by a culture that believed the creator should never abandon their creation. This item is cursed. Attuning to it extends the curse to you. As long as you remain cursed, you cannot remove the torque unless you are targeted by a Remove Curse spell or similar magic.

Orbital Lock. While you have at least one summoned creature active, you cannot voluntarily move more than 60 feet away from your nearest summoned creature. An invisible force of gravity prevents you from stepping beyond this radius.

The Snap. If you start your turn more than 60 feet away from your nearest summoned creature (for example, if the creature was forcefully moved or you were teleported), you are instantly dragged up to 30 feet in a straight line toward them. This forced movement provokes attacks of opportunity. Additionally, you take 1d10 force damage for every 10 feet traveled as the spatial tether strains against your body.

The artifact is a single, unadorned link of heavy, cold iron, its surface a dark, matte grey that seems to absorb the light. There is no visible seam or point of forging, and despite its apparent age, it bears no rust or corrosion. It feels unnervingly heavy in the hand, a weight that seems both physical and spiritual. This is not an item of overt power, but a devotional focus carried by the most dedicated followers of Letoria, the Chains of Judgement. It is said to serve as a constant reminder of the weight of the law and the burden of a just and merciful sentence. Legends claim such links are forged from the shackles of a truly repentant murderer or blessed in a secret rite that imbues the metal with the deity's unbending will for controlled, righteous judgment.

Weight of Conscience. While holding this link, the wielder can use a bonus action to present it and invoke Letoria's judgment upon one creature they can see within 60 feet. The target must succeed on a DC 16 Wisdom saving throw or be afflicted by the spiritual weight of their past transgressions. On a failed save, the creature takes 3d8 psychic damage and is Burdened by Penance until the end of its next turn. While burdened, the creature has disadvantage on all attack rolls and ability checks as its focus is shattered by guilt and self-recrimination. This property can be used once, and is regained daily at dawn.
Clarity of Judgment. While attuned to this item, the wielder has advantage on Wisdom (Insight) checks made to discern if a creature is lying or to ascertain its true intentions.

Final Penance. A creature of significant malice slain while under the effect of the Weight of Conscience finds its soul judged with immediate finality. Such a soul is irrevocably barred from being raised as any form of undead and resists all but the most powerful attempts at resurrection.

set.name: attunement
set.addition: by a paladin, cleric, or individual sworn to Letoria

description.p: This palm-sized pendant is carved from a non-reflective, petrified draconic material that seems to absorb the light around it. It depicts a dragon's face, not in aggression, but in serene, ancient contemplation, with its eyes closed. Worn on a simple, dark cord, it hangs heavy with the weight of cause and effect.

The Visage of Causality is an evolving artifact that resonates with the bearer's actions, awakening new powers as they align their deeds with the fundamental axioms of the Unnamed Deity it represents.
Dormant State. While in its dormant state, the Visage grants the following benefits:
Wyrm's Resolve. The pendant grants you a sliver of the Unnamed Wyrm's absolute certainty, fortifying your mind. You have advantage on saving throws against being charmed or frightened.

The Final Toll. You learn the Toll the Dead cantrip, which counts as a bard cantrip for you but doesn't count against the number of bard cantrips you know. When you cast it, the spectral bell toll is replaced by a low, draconic hum that resonates with the finality of existence. The power of the Visage enhances this cantrip for you: when you cast it, the spell is instead a d10 radiant damage when undamaged, and an additional +1 d12 radiant damage when they are injured (effectively one tier cantrip higher).

Awakening the Visage. The Visage awakens when you act as an agent of causality, ensuring a final, irreversible outcome. This is not an act of passion, but one of grim necessity. This can include, but is not limited to: ending the unnatural existence of a creature that has cheated death, ensuring its story truly concludes; destroying a powerful artifact whose function is to defy consequence; or making a monumental choice that sets a people or a nation on an irreversible path, and fully embracing the consequences.

sheet.description: See magic item card for full artifact abilities and lore.

description.p: Elegant chiton imbued with the power of Caeplithias. When under the Wet effect, gains increased durability.
While wearing this chiton, your base AC becomes 15 + your Dex Modifier. While wet this is increased to a base AC of 16 + your dex modifier.

This armor is fashioned from a base of Chimeric Hide, a leather harvested from experimental monstrosities that shifts its texture and color to match the environment. It is reinforced with Marrow-Steel bracers and studding, a white-veined alloy of pulverized bone and steel that is shockingly lightweight and porous. The Marrow-Steel components are etched with the Rune of the Unyielding Core, which hums faintly, dormant.

Chimeric Adaptation. While wearing this armor, you have advantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks made to hide in environments that match the hide's current texture or color. Roll a 1d10; on an even number your armor matches the terrain, on an odd it instead contrasts, giving you disadvantage.

Marrow-Steel Resonance. The lightweight nature of the Marrow-Steel allows this armor to be worn without a Strength requirement, and it does not impose disadvantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks despite its metallic studding. Additionally, it provides a +1 bonus to AC, and an additional +1 AC if you are surrounded entirely by bones.
Khar-gratt (Dormant). ????

This suit of scale mail is a heavy, imposing piece of craftsmanship that radiates a quiet menace. The upper breastplate is forged from a seamless, dark alloy where the Tempest-Vein ingot has fully integrated the steel of the consumed gauntlets. The surface is smooth but unnaturally cold, possessing a sheen that shifts between gunmetal grey and the deep blue of a bruised sky. It smells faintly of ozone and charred meat, the only lingering sign of the dragon-breath forge that birthed it.

The lower faulds are constructed from Scánarth scales, expertly cut and grafted into a flexible skirt of interlocking plates. They are laid with precision, creating a near-impenetrable barrier that moves fluidly with the wearer. Deliberate channels have been carved across the chest and shoulders, filled with dull Sirronium to form the shape of a "???? (TBD)" rune. These lines are clean and geometric, currently lying dormant and grey, waiting for the spark that will ignite their true purpose.

The Cold Grip. The armor absorbed Kadmon's Cocytus gauntlets during the forging process. The metal retains an unnatural chill which seems to shield you from intense heat. You have Resistance to Fire Damage.

Fulminous Veins. The Tempest-Vein ore within the alloy acts as a greedy lightning rod, drawing electrical energy into the Sirronium veins and granting you Resistance to Lightning Damage. This protection is not infinite. The armor can absorb a total of 40 points of Lightning Damage (calculated by the amount of damage prevented by the resistance). If this threshold is exceeded, the Sirronium veins glow white-hot with contained energy, and the dampening field temporarily collapses, causing you to lose this Resistance until you finish a Long Rest to let the energy dissipate.

Grafted Scánarth Plating. The Scánarth scales grafted to the lower faulds are incredibly dense and have been layered to create a surface where no blade can find purchase. When a creature scores a Critical Hit against you, you can use your reaction to angle the armor, causing the blow to skitter harmlessly across the hardened scales and turning the Critical Hit into a Normal Hit. You can use this feature once, and you regain the ability to do so after finishing a Long Rest.

Latent Instability. The Sirronium rune is currently dormant. However, if the Fulminous Veins limit is reached, the armor sheds bright light in a 20-foot radius and dim light for an additional 20 feet. This light persists for one hour. Stealth is practically impossible while this effect is active, and you are vulnerable to lightning damage during this time, but gain +2 AC as your armor is on the cusp of something greater.

This masterwork cuirass is a paradox of grim materials and profound care. It is composed of the slate-grey, stone-like chitinous plates of a Skordgur, yet it has been painstakingly shaped and articulated to fit a human frame, an act of incredible skill and empathy. The armor feels immensely solid and protective, a silent testament to the Forgemaster who sacrificed his own form to create it for his apprentice.

Etched into the surface are channels and mounting points, a clear framework designed to house arcane runes. While most are currently empty, a single, foundational Undorathi rune is inscribed upon the breastplate. Its groove is filled with a strange, unknown substance that pulses with a faint, contained light. This armor is not a finished masterpiece, but rather a perfect foundation, waiting for a new master to complete the work.

Forgemaster's Resolve. You have a +1 bonus to AC while wearing this armor. The armor has a pool of 3 charges. When you are hit by an attack from a creature you can see, you can use your reaction to expend 1 charge and reduce the damage you take by 1d10 + your proficiency bonus. The armor regains all expended charges when you finish a short or long rest spent in the warmth of a significant heat source, such as a forge or a large bonfire. During this time, you must tend to the armor, as if maintaining a forge, to rekindle its protective spirit.

Runic Matrix. The armor was designed as a conduit for runic power. It currently has one active rune slot, which can be used to house any rune you know. A rune inscribed in this armor is more potent than normal; its passive effect is enhanced, as determined by the GM.

Foundational Rune. The armor bears a single, foundational Undorathi Rune of Shaping inscribed by its creator. This rune is not merely a carving; it is believed to be an echo of the forgemaster's own soul, a final lesson in creation and purpose. While this rune is active, you are proficient with smith's tools.

Awakening Legacy. This cuirass is an unfinished work of immense potential. Its true power is dormant and can be awakened through multiple stages of evolution. Unlocking these stages will require a combination of factors: infusing the armor with more of the strange substance used in its creation, which the forgemaster may have had imbued in himself, achieving personal mastery over new runic forms, and performing deeds of great significance worthy of its creator's sacrifice. Each new stage of its evolution will increase the number of runes the armor can hold and may unlock entirely new magical properties.
Imbued Plate. You have a +1 bonus to AC while wearing this armor.
Renowned Presence. While wearing this armor, you have advantage on Charisma checks when engaged with individuals knowledgeable on the "Forgotten King" (You need to discover who this is).

Memory of Resilience. Once per long rest, you can conjure a shimmering shield that grants you resistance to one type of magical or elemental damage as a reaction for one minute. When using this feature make a DC 20 Constitution saving throw. On a success, you fully negate the damage type, but can never attempt to resist or negate that type again. The armor will change to reflect this success. On a failure, you just take regular resisted damage.
A javelin crackling with contained storm energy. It counts as a magical weapon for any attack purposes.

Stats. You gain a +2 bonus to attack rolls made with this magic weapon. It deals Lightning damage instead of piercing or any other damage type added to the attack. Immediately after you make a ranged attack with this javelin, it vanishes in a flash of lightning and reappears in your hand.

Thunderous Arrival. Once per day, when you throw this javelin, you can speak its command word. Whether the attack hits or misses, the javelin erupts in a deafening peal of thunder upon reaching its target's location. The target and all creatures within 10 feet of it must make a Constitution saving throw or take 2d8 Thunder damage and be deafened for one minute.

Stormstruck. The javelin acts as a natural lightning rod. If you are targeted by an effect that deals lightning damage, you can use your reaction to hold the javelin aloft. You take no damage from the effect, and the javelin absorbs the entirety of the electrical charge. If within a storm, the property will still take effect, but you will take damage from the lightning strike. You may use the reactive portion of this effect once per long rest.

Tempest's Fury. If the javelin has absorbed a lightning effect using its Lightning Rod property, or if you are fighting in the midst of a natural thunderstorm and are struck by lightning, the weapon becomes supercharged. Its silvery-blue veins crackle audibly with contained power, and it sheds dim light in a 10-foot radius. While in this state, the javelin's bonus to attack and damage rolls increases to +4, and it deals an additional 1d10 lightning damage on every hit. This supercharged state lasts for 10 minutes after absorbing a spell, or as long as the natural storm rages. Y'volr is resistant to all lightning strike and lightning magic based effects during this ability.
Won as a reward in an arena by Aurea, this weapon was left behind in the Abomination lair.

Cursed. Upon death, your body will guard this weapon, and force whoever wishes to claim it from you to succeed in a difficult trial. The trial will vary based upon how you die, and the condition your body is in.

Stats. You gain a +2 bonus to attack rolls made with this magic weapon. The weapon's base damage die is altered to 1d10 (or 1d12 if wielded with two hands). On a hit, the weapon deals an additional 2 necrotic damage and 2 cold damage. Additionally, you topple enemies on a critical hit.

Osseous Grasp. You gain 1 use of this ability per long rest. Up to 3 creatures within 60 feet of you must succeed an Intelligence saving throw, else they are restrained by skeletal hands that grasp them from all over. Any creature caught in this counts as grappled, and must succeed a saving throw in order to take their turn. Every subsequent failed saving throw drains 1d4 + your ability modifier cold damage from the target. The caster regains half this damage as HP. Each round the caster maintains this spell after the initial cast, they are required to also succeed a saving throw, else fall victim to their own spell. If the caster fails, they are trapped until they succeed a saving throw.
Also known as "Mother's Sword", this weapon seems to gain power from each plight you slay, perhaps requiring more than one kill to fully awaken its potential. It can be used as a spellcasting focus.
Stats. This weapon's base damage is 1d10 Piercing damage. On a hit, it deals an additional 2 force damage and 2 psychic damage.
Unworthy. If wielded while unworthy, you have disadvantage on all attacks made with this weapon, and you may randomly combust.
Augments. The sword gains more powers and modifiers as you complete your plights. Currently active augments:

ul.li: Hilt: You can cast the Eldritch Blast cantrip (manifesting as purple energy). You use your Wisdom modifier for this spell, and it includes the effects of the Grasp of Hadar invocation.
Abyssal (Blade): Represents the completed Abyssal (Gilded) plight. Grants the blade its +2 Psychic Damage.

description.p: Said to be the favored weapon of King Brect of Dothoren, Sunsplitter played a pivotal role in the founding of what would later be recognized as the Kingdom of Dothoren. It is believed to have been blessed by the Blazing Jewel, a radiant deity who represents the Sun, and formerly justice. While not the subject of worship, being chosen by such a divine entity is considered an extraordinary honor. Legends speak of how Sunsplitter was wielded in the legendary Battle of Dawn's Light, where King Brect vanquished a dark army threatening his kingdom. Formerly, it was passed down through the royal lineage as a symbol of hope and courage in times of darkness. Now it finds itself in the hands of a new paragon, and potential competitor for the throne.
Stats. This weapon deals 2d8 Piercing damage and possesses the finesse property. It grants a +4 bonus to attack rolls made with it, and its damage rolls gain a +4 Radiant damage bonus.
Divine Ward. While wielding this weapon, you gain a +2 bonus to your Armor Class.

Blazing Strike. On a hit, this weapon unleashes a wave of radiant energy. The target must make a DC 19 Constitution saving throw, taking an additional 2d8 radiant damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one. This can only occur when the sun is currently up. If the sun is down, the effect instead becomes Extinguishing Strike.

Extinguishing Strike. On a hit, this weapon unleashes a wave of radiant energy. The target must make a DC 19 Wisdom saving throw, taking an additional 2d8 necrotic damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one. This can only occur when the moon is currently up. If the sun is down, the effect instead becomes Blazing Strike.
Tempered Fervor. During absolute dusk or absolute dawn, neither Blazing Strike nor Extinguishing Strike can activate.

Jewel's Radiance. As a bonus action, the wielder can activate the Sun's Radiance feature once per long rest. This envelops the weapon in dazzling light for 1 minute, causing any creature that starts its turn within 10 feet of the wielder to make a DC 19 Dexterity saving throw, taking 4d6 radiant damage on a failed save and becoming blinded until the end of its next turn. Creatures that succeed on the save take half damage and are not blinded.

Radiant Crown. Once per day, you can invoke the power of Sunsplitter to cast the Sunbeam spell (spell save DC 19). When you do so, the area of effect shimmers with golden light, forcing all hostile creatures within the radius to make a Constitution saving throw or take 8d8 radiant damage and be blinded for one minute. A creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the blindness on a success.

Borne from the material laden mind, this sickle is forged from a mixed alloy of Blister-Steel and Ghost-Steel. Crafted for the purpose of rending enemies gaunt, it excels in strikes made from the shadows. With a Serum-Soaked Plank handle, wrapped in Vessel-Cloth, it also provides a satisfying flexibility when swung harshly.
Properties. Nick, Light
Flexible Grip. You gain a +1 bonus to attack rolls made with this weapon.

Shadow-Stich. This weapon is elusive to unsuspecting opponents. When striking from stealth, the jagged blades bite and gnaw at exposed flesh, leaving a vicious wound behind. The target takes 1d4 necrotic damage at the start of each turn. Any additional strike with advantage continues to tear away at their vulnerability, causing an additional 1d4 necrotic damage for each hit received by this weapon before their next turn. After 1 minute, or upon receiving a healing spell that would close wounds, this effect ends.
Rat Rune. You can communicate simple ideas to rodents, and will be seen slightly more favorable for each true rune you have on your person.
!Krell-Valak (Dormant). ????

This massive, heavy mace consists of a dense, matte black iron core wrapped in intricate silver bands that hum with latent energy. The head is connected to a sturdy staff by a length of reddish, hell-forged chain. Jagged spikes made of a volatile, unstable metal protrude from the striking surface, while the handle is carved from a single piece of dark, violet glass.

When left unattended, it is said that the screams of those who fell victim to one of the weapon's components can still be heard... the red glow of the spikes illuminating the hellish chain attached to it.
Stats. You gain a bonus to your damage rolls with this magic weapon equal to 1d6 force damage.

Sundering Strikes. The weight of the black iron core combined with the corrosive nature of the spikes makes this weapon devastating against defenses. When you hit a creature wearing or carrying nonmagical metal armor or a nonmagical metal shield, the object takes a permanent and cumulative -1 penalty to the AC it offers. Armor reduced to an AC of 10 or a shield that drops to a +0 bonus is destroyed. On a nat 20, your blow reduces the AC by -3 instead of -1.

Kinetic Extension. The hell-metal chain allows the maul head to be swung with increased momentum. Once per turn, you can extend the reach of an attack with this weapon to 10 feet. If you hit a creature with an extended attack, they must succeed on a DC 15 Strength saving throw or be knocked prone.
Unstable Flare (Dormant). ????

Forged from the crystallized, corrupted blood of Lady Ovidia Morvelle, this magnificent two-handed scythe features an impossibly sharp, crimson glass blade. It is unnervingly heavy, cold to the touch, and hums with a faint pressure that seems to swallow all ambient noise in the room.
You gain a +2 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon. When you hit a creature with it, the target takes an extra 1d6 necrotic damage.

Crescendo's End. The scythe holds 3 charges and regains 1d3 expended charges daily at dawn. When you hit a creature with a melee weapon attack using this scythe, you can expend 1 charge to deal an additional 2d8 necrotic damage. The glass blade leaves a parasitic vibration on the target's throat. The first time the target attempts to cast a spell with a verbal component before the start of your next turn, they must make a DC 16 Charisma saving throw. On a failed save, the spell is entirely absorbed into the scythe. The spell fails, but the spell slot is still expended.

Thunderous Expulsion. If the scythe successfully absorbs a spell in this way, the glass blade glows with volatile kinetic energy. On your next successful melee attack with the scythe within 1 minute, it forcefully expels this trapped magic. The target takes an additional 1d8 thunder damage per level of the absorbed spell (or 1d8 for a cantrip), and the stored energy is cleared.

Tainted Vitality (Curse). The childhood poison that stunted Ovidia's magic still courses through the crystallized blood. While attuned to this weapon, your voice is supernaturally muffled. You cannot be heard from more than 30 feet away, and any attempt to shout or scream simply comes out at a normal, conversational volume. Furthermore, the corrupted blood violently rejects spoken magic. Whenever you cast a spell of 1st level or higher with a verbal component, you take 1d4 poison damage per level of the spell cast.

The Silent Sovereign (Sentience). Ovidia's Blood-Glass Scythe is a sentient weapon of neutral evil alignment. It shares the mental ability scores of its creator. The weapon cannot speak, but it communicates a chilling, silent pressure to its wielder. It possesses a lingering echo of Lady Morvelle's will, and it often feels as though the scythe is simply biding its time, quietly yearning to return to the hands that bled it into existence. It is fickle and judgmental; depending entirely on its inscrutable mood, the scythe has been known to mysteriously guide the wielder's swing to grant advantage on a crucial strike, or suddenly grow unnaturally heavy, imposing disadvantage when it feels displeased.

This heavy staff is carved from the petrified root of a primordial tree. Two metallic serpents, one forged of tarnished silver and the other of dull gold, are coiled tightly around the ancient wood. The metal is cold to the touch, yet the serpents occasionally shift and slide along the shaft of their own accord. At the base, the petrified wood splinters into jagged, dormant tendrils that hum faintly when pressed against the earth.

Primordial Focus. You gain a +2 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic quarterstaff. While holding it, you also gain a +2 bonus to spell attack rolls and to the saving throw DCs of your spells. The staff holds 5 charges and regains 1d4 expended charges daily at dawn.

The First Guide's Mercy. You have advantage on Wisdom (Medicine) checks. Furthermore, whenever you cast a spell of 1st level or higher that restores hit points to a creature, the golden serpent sheds a faint light, granting that creature temporary hit points equal to your spellcasting ability modifier.

The Silver Slumber. When you hit a creature with a melee attack using this staff, you can choose to forgo all damage from the strike and instead expend 1 charge to command the silver serpent to bite. The serpent injects a profound, magical anesthetic. The target must succeed on a DC 15 Constitution saving throw or fall completely unconscious for one minute. The creature wakes early if it takes any damage or if someone uses an action to shake or slap it awake. If the target was struck with advantage, they must make the saving throw with disadvantage.

The Golden Toll. As an action, you can expend 2 charges to press the golden serpent against the skin of a willing creature. The serpent bites the target, instantly curing it of one disease or one condition afflicting it. The condition can be blinded, deafened, paralyzed, or poisoned. However, this medicine requires a steep price. The target must immediately expend one of its own Hit Dice, rolling it and taking unavoidable necrotic damage equal to the result.

The Primordial Chrysalis. As an action, you can expend 5 charges to forcefully plant the base of the staff into the ground. Ethereal roots erupt from the wood, anchoring the staff in place and tightly wrapping around the corpse of a creature within 15 feet of you. The roots coil like massive twin serpents, forming a glowing, impenetrable chrysalis around the fallen ally. For the next minute, you must maintain concentration on this effect as if casting a spell, and you cannot let go of the staff, nor move it in any way. While channeling, you gain the following benefits:

ul.li: Dedicated Latros: You have advantage on Constitution saving throws made to maintain concentration. You also have advantage on any saving throw or ability check made to resist being forcefully moved.

Surging Vitality: The staff automatically radiates the magic of the Aura of Vitality spell. For the duration of the channel, you can use your bonus action on each of your turns to restore 2d6 hit points to one creature you choose within 30 feet of the staff. Additionally, you can cast Mass Healing Word as an action, at will.
The Serpent's Ward: The creature resting inside the root chrysalis is completely immune to all damage and magical effects from anything other than the full might of the Divine.

description.p: The Rebirth. If you successfully maintain concentration for the full minute, the roots dissolve into a lingering Aura of Life that wraps around the target. The creature is instantly brought back to life with all its hit points restored, and it is cured of all diseases and conditions (such as poisoned, blinded, or paralyzed). To protect the mind from the shock of resurrection, the creature immediately falls into a magical, coma-like stasis for 24 hours. If your concentration is broken, or if you are ever forcefully moved more than 5 feet away from the staff, the channeling shatters. The chrysalis turns to ash, and the target remains dead.

Dormancy. Whether the ritual succeeds or fails, invoking the First Guide's ultimate mercy drains the staff entirely. Once the minute passes or the channel breaks, the staff goes dormant. For exactly seven days, it becomes a mundane quarterstaff. It loses its +2 bonuses to attacks, damage, and spell save DCs, and it cannot regain charges until the seventh dawn has passed.

Hauke's Reach has been passed down through generations of the Hauke noble family, said to be blessed by the spirit of the dragon Ice Yterin. Legend claims that the first wielder, Lady Arisel Hauke, defeated a terrible beast that plagued her lands by channeling the ancestral powers through this very glaive. Its legacy lives on as a symbol of honor and protection for those who wield it. Stories of its use in heroic deeds circulate among the bards, inspiring many to seek out its power. Only those who have had this weapon directly passed down to them may wield it, typically as a transfer from parent to child.
Stats. This weapon functions as a standard glaive, but on a hit, it deals an additional 2 piercing damage and 2 cold damage.

Breathe of Yterin. Once per day, when you hit a creature with this glaive, you can choose to unleash the force of Yterin's breath upon your foe. This creates a 30-foot cone of ice, dealing 6d6 cold damage. The affected creature must make a DC 19 Dexterity saving throw, taking half damage on a success. If they fail the save, they are chilled to the bone, causing them to lose half their movement speed for 1 minute.

set.name: attunement
set.addition: by a direct heir of the Hauke family

element.name: Aethelgard, Last Light of The Forgotten Sky

description.p: This weapon appears to be an exquisitely crafted longsword, forged from a single piece of luminous white steel that seems to possess its own internal light. In stark contrast, its hilt is an ornate yet purposeful work of a seamless, matte black metal, cool to the touch. The material's light-absorbing quality and immense density are reminiscent of the mythical ore Argenox, leading some to wonder if this is a purer, more primal form of the material, created outside of Nelvora before the "Heaven's Tear" was ever shaped by this world's influence.

At the very center of the crossguard is one standout symbol: a circular sigil depicting the life rings of a great tree, sundered by a clean crack down the middle. On one half, the rings are perfect and symmetrical, glowing with a faint, internal light. On the other, the rings are warped into chaotic knots and whorls from which a single, dark, thorny shape grows. The weapon's true nature is betrayed by its impossible mass; while shaped like a longsword, its weight is immense, far exceeding that of even a mundane greatsword. Found within the ruins of a forgotten sanctum in Salusare, it is a vestige that has been long orphaned from its true home and true purpose.

In the hands of one with a true divine calling, Aethelgard forms a powerful bond. When the wielder channels their will into the weapon, the physical blade acts as the core, erupting in a sheath of semi-translucent, solid light that forms its true shape: a shimmering, vibrant extension that brings the weapon to its full greatsword size and reach. It is theorized that this projected blade is a memory of the weapon's possible original form, its immense mass finally aligning with its awakened purpose. To wield it is to carry fragments of a legacy long lost to time... or simply erased. There may exist some who would scorn the burden enshrined within this weapon, or even those who would seek to destroy it.

Before Attunement. To a creature not attuned to it, Aethelgard is treated as a masterwork longsword. Due to its impossible mass, any attack roll made with it has disadvantage. It is considered a non-magical weapon in this state. Attuning to the weapon is a process that requires a full day of meditation and communion with the weapon's dormant will, after which its other properties are unlocked for you. This meditation must be conducted in a place of divine significance, or amidst the carnage of a battlefield.

White-Steel Blade. The otherworldly material of this weapon is exceptionally forged. You have a +4 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon. This bonus increases by an additional +2 during the effects of Radiant Awakening.

Awakened Form. Upon attuning to this weapon, its dormant power partially awakens. The physical blade acts as the core for a blade of semi-translucent, solid light that extends its form. For you, Aethelgard is always treated as a magical greatsword with the following properties:

ul.li: It has the reach property.
It sheds bright light in a 20-foot radius and dim light for an additional 20 feet.
It deals an extra 2d4 radiant damage to any target it hits.

description.p: Radiant Awakening. The weapon's deepest protective instincts are its own and cannot be commanded. If you are reduced to 0 hit points but not killed outright while holding this weapon, Aethelgard unleashes a burst of furious light. You are immediately restored to 1 hit point. In addition, each hostile creature within 30 feet of you must make a DC 18 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 10d8 radiant damage and is blinded until the end of your next turn. On a successful save, a creature takes half as much damage and is not blinded. This property cannot be used again for at least 30 days. It may take longer for it to recover, depending on conditions outside your current knowledge.

The Withdrawn Light. For the 30 days that Radiant Awakening is on cooldown, the weapon's nature changes. The matte black metal of the hilt seems to creep over the blade in a protective, light-absorbing shell. During this time, the weapon has the following properties:

ul.li: The extra damage it deals changes from 2d4 radiant to 2d8 necrotic damage. When you deal this necrotic damage to a target, you take necrotic damage equal to the amount dealt. You cannot reduce the damage taken to yourself by any means.
The light it sheds is replaced by a 10-foot radius of magical darkness. This darkness blinds you and any other creature that starts its turn within it.

As a bonus action, you can draw upon the weapon's sorrow to see. You take 10d8 necrotic damage that cannot be reduced by any means, and in exchange, you can see through this magical darkness for 1 hour. You can use this feature once per long rest.

While the weapon is in this state, you are constantly afflicted with feelings of paranoia and violent emotions. You have disadvantage on Wisdom (Insight) checks and Charisma (Persuasion) checks. Any other creature that ends its turn within the weapon's magical darkness must succeed on a DC 17 Wisdom saving throw or feel their sanity begin to fray, the creature has disadvantage on all Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma saving throws for the next minute.

description.p: Dormant Sigils. The grey sigils along the blade's fuller are currently inert. Their purpose is unknown, but they glow with a soft, white light when Radiant Awakening is active, suggesting they are tied to an even greater, undiscovered power.

The Unspoken Name. The true name of the weapon, or a command that would unlock its full potential, is lost to time. You have a persistent, intuitive sense that discovering this name and speaking it would awaken the dormant sigils, transforming the weapon into its final, most powerful form and perhaps granting you control over its light and shadow aspects.

set.name: attunement
set.addition: by a creature with a divine blessing or calling

description.p: Officially titled the Chaos Blade (Blade of Friendship) or the Abyssal Cleaver Reforged, this bizarre weapon is best known simply as "Zerik's Sword." When you are distracted or resting, the sword will constantly try to descend into the ground. It might be seeking comfort, or perhaps seeking something else entirely.

Abyssal Magnet. The blade attracts Abyssal enemies, but grants great prowess against them as well. Weaker Abyssal creatures must make a Charisma check to even approach you, or become charmed if they start their turn within 30 feet.

Chaotic Strikes. This weapon uses Charisma for its attack rolls, regardless of your class. It deals a base 2d8 slashing damage, plus an additional 2 fire damage on a hit. However, its combat logic is completely inverted. You take a -2 penalty to attack rolls against standard AC, and you have disadvantage on attacks against Abyssal creatures.
Inverted Reality. In order to successfully hit an enemy, your attack roll must equal or be lower than 0 minus the enemy's Charisma modifier.

Catastrophic Criticals. You score a critical hit against Abyssal creatures on a roll of 2 or 3. If you roll a natural 1, the sword repeats your critical damage a second time as a balanced mixture of lightning and fire damage. If this attack kills the target, the additional damage courses to adjacent Abyssal creatures.
Haukr's Forgeheart. Thanks to the pommel's heavy grounding, you have advantage on saving throws or ability checks against being displaced forcefully.

Earthshatter. Once per long rest, as an action, you can slam the pommel into the ground. This unleashes a shockwave of force and splintered earth. Each creature in a 15-foot cone originating from you must make a Dexterity saving throw. A creature takes 3d8 bludgeoning damage on a failed save, and half as much damage on a successful one. The area of the cone becomes difficult terrain until cleared. This ability may also affect you: roll a d20. On an even number, you are affected by the shockwave; on an odd number, you are unaffected.

For the 30 days that Aethelgard's Radiant Awakening is on cooldown, the weapon's nature changes. The matte black metal of the hilt creeps over the blade in a protective, light-absorbing shell. It retains its impossible mass and reach, but its divine light is replaced by a sorrowful, parasitic hunger.
White-Steel Blade (Dimmed). You retain the +4 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.
The Withdrawn Light. While in this cursed state, the weapon has the following properties:

ul.li: The extra damage it deals is 2d8 necrotic damage. When you deal this necrotic damage to a target, you take necrotic damage equal to the amount dealt. You cannot reduce the damage taken to yourself by any means.
The weapon sheds a 10-foot radius of magical darkness. This darkness blinds you and any other creature that starts its turn within it.

As a bonus action, you can draw upon the weapon's sorrow to see. You take 10d8 necrotic damage that cannot be reduced by any means, and in exchange, you can see through this magical darkness for 1 hour. You can use this feature once per long rest.
You are constantly afflicted with feelings of paranoia and violent emotions. You have disadvantage on Wisdom (Insight) checks and Charisma (Persuasion) checks.

Any other creature that ends its turn within the weapon's magical darkness must succeed on a DC 17 Wisdom saving throw or feel their sanity begin to fray. A failing creature has disadvantage on all Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma saving throws for the next minute.

set.name: attunement
set.addition: by a creature with a divine blessing or calling

element.name: The Aegis of the Twin Lions

description.p: This magnificent heater shield of ancient Fiscathian design is crafted from dark ironwood faced with burnished steel, its front dominated by a masterfully sculpted bas-relief of two roaring silver lions, the ancient symbols of House Rochefort. It is said to have been borne by Lord Theron Rochefort, "The Unyielding," during the legendary Siege of the Crimson Ford. Despite its size, the Aegis feels remarkably balanced.
Armor Class. You have a +1 bonus to your Armor Class while you are wielding this shield. This is in addition to the shield's normal +2 bonus.

Lion's Guard. The Aegis seems to preternaturally deflect blows. When an attacker that you can see hits you with an attack roll, you can use your reaction to add your proficiency bonus to your AC for that attack, potentially causing the attack to miss you. The air around the shield often shimmers subtly when a strike is imminent. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus, regaining all expended uses when you finish a long rest.

Unyielding Stance. When a creature you can see attacks an ally within 10 feet of you, you can use your reaction to redirect the attack to yourself, interposing the Aegis with uncanny effectiveness. You must choose to do so before the attack roll is made. This ability draws upon your deep focus and can be used once per short or long rest.

Echo of Courage (Rumored). Legends persist that the indomitable spirit within the Aegis can lend its resilience. If you are reduced to 0 hit points by an attacker you can see but not killed outright, the shield may flare with a brief, warm inner light. If this occurs, you drop to 1 hit point instead. Votary scholars believe this profound intervention is a rare and sacred occurrence, drawing deeply from the shield's ancient reserves of protective energy. (DM Note: This feature might activate once per long rest, or under specific narrative conditions of dire peril when upholding a protective vow.)

set.name: attunement
set.addition: by an individual dedicated to martial discipline or protective vows
